item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It said -2 was impaired in ASL because of webs, but some of them have full paws. Can they move their fingers for ASL? That still doesn't address the ones with hooves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shoot, I thought I'd already done that. Moved now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it's good to have content warnings, putting them three clicks behind the primary content of the work (two of those said clicks giving no indication that they will lead to content warnings) makes them hard to reach to the point of defeating having them at all. I think that they should really be moved into the main section of the info module, rather than the more section - the primary section certainly isn't bloated at present, in any case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ A Measured Response
– hide block

🤔

item.title: i pissed my self
item.content:encoded: i literally pissed my self reading this, good job.

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Wow, congrats! I really liked this one, it was really well written :-) .

!!!!!

This is my first skip to hit 200. Thanks y'all, feels good. Here's to 300?

item.title: Re: Just a question/request.
item.content:encoded:
Neither exist, that I know of. The only continuation of this story is the tale After That, Until Then by psul (linked in a reply to my author post), and I don't personally intend to do any more with it for the foreseeable future.

That said, if anyone sees this and is inspired I would be psyched to see what they produce!

item.title: Just a question/request.
item.content:encoded: Does anyone know if there is a tale relating to Brian and the cult? Does the picture of the sketch of Brian exist? If those are no's id like someone to make a few. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love every thing about this. I love this amazing twist at the end with such strange and phenomenal characters. I think it works because of the detail and excellent writing style. It perfectly fits in with the style of the foundation. Super creative.

This is definitely one of my favorites.

item.title: Just in case anyone is confused as I am about Tab 2's logs
item.content:encoded:
I arranged them in timestamp order.

+ show block
– hide block

CONTAINMENT CELL: 1100: SCP-███ is dormant in its tank.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1123: SCP-███ begins to move slowly towards the top of its tank.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1130: An alarm sounds from another sector. SCP-███ spins in place and begins to move quickly, attempting to move through the glass of its tank. This activates a small alarm, which is ignored due to an emergency in E block. SCP-███ is unable to move through the glass.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1130: Two armed men enter the common room.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1131: SCP-███ rams the glass of its tank, forming a crack. SCP-███ rams again, breaking the glass. Water floods containment chamber. SCP begins to move in fast circles, stirring the water as it drains.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1131: Men begin to open common room doors leading to bedrooms and fire within.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1132: SCP-███ swims upwards, moving through the ceiling of the containment chamber.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1132: A woman appears in the entrance to the common room. She is on the ground, and bleeding profusely. The woman calls out.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1133: Two children run out from an unopened door, towards the woman. Both men turn and gun down the children. One man returns to opening bedroom doors, while the other approaches the woman and shoots her in the throat.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1134: A shadow is cast over the room. Several dozen metre-long tentacles move through the wall and begin exploring the room, moving toward the gunmen. They reach the first man and wrap around him. He calls out.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1135: The second man turns around, raising his gun. A single tentacle moves quickly, through his brain. He falls to the ground silently.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1136: SCP-███ enters the room, obscuring the camera.
E-BLOCK LOW SECURITY DORM: 1137: SCP exits the room, leaving behind only the bodies of the two gunmen.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1140: SCP-███ returns through the ceiling of the containment chamber and moves to the central point of the room.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1201: Drains are opened in containment chamber, allowing flooded water to subside.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1205: Several personnel step into SCP-███'s chamber and converse among themselves.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1234: SCP-███ moves downwards, toward the personnel. They begin to panic.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1235: SCP-███ deposits 23 bodies on the floor and returns to the centre point of the room.
CONTAINMENT CELL: 1310: Personnel leave containment chamber with bodies on stretchers.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. Got a simple one here. The tale After That/Until Then heavily features SCP-2338 and could use a direct link from its parent SCP. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

When asked to spell this, SCP-2338-B fingerspelled E-O-M-I (어미), a Korean word meaning "incubator" or "mother".

To this:

When asked to spell this, SCP-2338-B fingerspelled E-O-M-I (어미), a Korean word meaning "incubator" or "mother".

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, I don't know why, but the fact that the children were/are all in animal costumes that don't come off is absolutely terrifying/creepy af. Where did you come up with this stuff. Wow. And ew.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap…. this gave me chills.
I absolutely love this scip, and it's probably going down in history as my favourite SCP to date. Good work!!

For the record, I thought the details about the individual children was great. It gave them a sense of individuality, and made them into more than just subjects.

+1 (wish I could give +100)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shut up and take my upvotes!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cried so hard after reading this. Take my upvote and my heart

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me want to cry. Also seconding Pastarasta on the SCP2419 crosslink being very confusing considering that it is now the Laughing Men

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since SCP-2419 has since been removed and rewritten, shouldn't the number 1 footnote be removed or rewritten?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super late addition, realised I didn't give attribution for the jellyfish image used in the article. It's from this wikipedia page, and is licensed under CC BY 2.0, which allows me to both share and adapt it.

item.title: Re: A Tale!
item.content:encoded: Thanks Rimple - although I feel a bit bad for cutting off potential continuation from the author. Feel free to write it anyway - there is no canon!

item.title: A Tale!
item.content:encoded: This skip now has a follow-up tale, After That/Until Then, written by psul. It serves as a sequel, but also a sort of reimagining and wrap-up of the events in this article, complementing it really nicely. I consider it canon, and don't intend to ever do my own continuation of Sun-Hee's story, so if you want to see more, it's well worth checking out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a brother named Mikey.
This hit me hard in the feels.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great work. I appreciate the depth that you gave each kiddo. Giving them a whole backstory and just, HUMANITY, builds a fantastic hunk of meat to push that knife into when scary times come around.

One thing: Did I miss something that noted the hormonal regulation that the jellyfish might give, or is that just an incidental thing. Maybe the creature interacts with the child's metabolism to 'fix' a mild aberration? Seems interesting regardless, but the How of the process digs at me.

+1

item.title: Re: 어미?
item.content:encoded:
I really appreciate how much depth you've gone into here, especially with how busy you must be. The problem is I don't have an alternate that will serve the same emotional final punch - ultimately, I'm stretching here because it seriously serves the story, and the sacrifice vs the payoff is worth it right now. Obviously, though, I'd like not to have that sacrifice at all, so if you ever think of something that will serve the same purpose but with more accuracy, I'd love for you to let me know.

I hope everything goes great with you and your kid ^^

item.title: Re: 어미?
item.content:encoded:
Heh, you're describing me, a second-generation American child of working-class Korean immigrants. I'm not certain how familiar you are with child development but it's standard for most children to say some derivation of "mother" as their first intentional word by 6-12 months (have a 2.5 mo newborn so this is off of top of head). Unless the parents were adamantly against using the language they are most familiar with (it is not unheard of for Korean parents to want their children to be fully Americanized by not teaching them Korean), it would be hard to avoid the deluge of children songs, TV shows, books, not to mention family and community that often refer to 엄마.

It is also customary for immigrant parents who both work, to recruit a family member, who is most often the grandmother, to be the child's caregiver. It would be very unusual for Korean immigrants to be leaning on an English-speaking daycare service before the age of 4 (preschool becomes available for free/nominal charge).

I do know of some Korean-Americans who had lost the ability to comprehend/speak Korean but invariably, they still know the one word. I can appreciate the double meaning of the Eo-mi but I'm really stretching to work with the appropriate term of address, where a kid would hear and retain the term, AND understand the secondary definition if she wasn't formally or informally trained. Koreans family terms may seem convoluted but they're meant to denote appropriate levels of respect and quickly convey who-is-related-to-who (i.e., go-mo vs sue-mo are both aunt, but it conveys maternal vs paternal, and an adult would only use that term with a child in the context of how the child would address that aunt).

I'm not sure how big of a Korean-American audience you'd be catering to here so I'm willing to accept it with no vote. But it is a lot like a English-speaking child voluntarily calling this foster mother, "parent". Thanks for hearing me out.

Edit: Americanized kids section
Edit 2: seeing a bunch of grammatical errors but hopefully I got a point across. Pleading leniency as a new 엄마, sleep-deprived in this nonstop all-nighter called raising a baby.

item.title: Re: 어미?
item.content:encoded:
I actually talked this over with Quarks-3, a member of the -KO wiki. We eventually decided on eom-mi to retain the double-meaning of "incubator"(it was originally 보육기which I was told was totally wrong).

Sun-hee's Korean isn't the best, as a second-generation immigrant who learned it at home from parents both working full-time jobs and hasn't had proper practice in more than three years (an oversight on the Foundation's part, though they really are trying their best).

Given that, I thought it would be OK. Does that seen reasonable, or is this something that requires only the most basic grasp of Korean to understand? I don't speak it at all myself, so I've relied on the advice of those who do.

item.title: 어미?
item.content:encoded:
A child would call her mom Eom-ma 엄마.
The only persons who would use eom-mi are the people who are the same age or older than the mother, more often by a grandparent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found a possible mistake: -20 is described as both wearing gloves and having her hands painted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor edit made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All instances of SCP-2338 lack noses, ears and mouths.

Given that, shouldn't the following include "breathe"?

Despite this, subjects show an unhindered capacity to smell, hear and taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely something I intend to play with in the future, yeah. Foundation is cold, not cruel, and I don't think the "Not cruel" aspect is focused on half enough. In the mean-time, feel free to explore E-Block yourself ^^ Low-level security for safe humanoids, particularly child humanoids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very much intentional, as alluded to elsewhere in the comments. Glad this struck a note with you. Hope none of you or yours have any connection to Sandy Hook or similar incidents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After the emotional gut punch I realized the casualty numbers closely matched the Sandy Hook shootings (I'm from CT) and I felt like I had been slapped.

This is really good, thank you for writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit late on replying, but thank you for the clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a tale in there somewhere. Maybe even a whole canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
with standard Foundation curriculum for child humanoid anomalies

My favorite part of this is that it implies the Foundation has enough anomalous humans on their hands - children, even - that it has to have an entire education plan for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Blank, smooth skin.

here's a sketch I drew of Sun-Hee during the creative process, for reference
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's distinctly possible that I'm just being dense, but when the children are referred to as having no noses, mouths, or ears, are we talking "blank, smooth skin" or "gaping hole" where the missing parts should be? Due to the mouth part, I'm inclined to think the former, but if I could get confirmation one way or the other, I'd appreciate it, if only to have a mental image appropriate for what the author intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(I still wanna fuckin' know what the hell was up with Brian and the pictures and just… everything about that damned cult. c.c)

Don't worry, there IS backstory there. I may make a tale or something at a later date. I've already got an article planned that could potentially tie in with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -7 is odd enough that I might be parsing it wrong, but I think you refer to it as -17 once during its description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Running commentary
– hide block

Okay, but the raccoon kid's last name is Lotor? <.< The platypus kid is named Perry? Come on.

I feel like every usage of "child" in the description log should be replaced with 'instance'. Also, there are quite a few typos, most especially the one regarding the back half of the dragon.

Did you ever hear anyone being referred to as Brian?

Strikes me as odd, could work in parlance, not familiar with ASL lingo.

The kids started screaming

Also strikes me as odd.

Hah, I noticed the description of Jenny had a 'was' in there. Clever. I also like the slow unveiling of why Sato is having trouble speaking. (Of course, given that they all died, more or less, why only the single past-tense?)

…Okay, so the second article is wholly unrelated? Are we like, seeing the revisions of the page or something? Why is it measured in feet? The camera feed log is really bizarre, it just doesn't sound right.

…Okay, what in the fuck. Somehow, all this weirdness culminates in a punchline worth an upvote.

(I still wanna fuckin' know what the hell was up with Brian and the pictures and just… everything about that damned cult. c.c)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude. Dude. Thank you so much, jesus. You're so damn nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keep them all!

I loved that you took the time to give every single child at least a little due, each with a name, and it really helped sell the reach of the skip. I also enjoyed reading about the details of each costume, even if in the long run it really didn't matter, narratively. You keep it as it is, mister! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I strongly disagree.

For me, Rimple nailed what they were shooting for. Somewhere around -10 or -11 I settled into the groove of a fairly decent "collection-of-odd-things" SCP with a darkly whimsical vibe I rather liked, and around -15 I was really starting to get invested in them both as a group and as individuals. The interviews went on to paint some nice little character vignettes on top of that, and had me teetering on a "yeah, okay. This is good creepy-cute, I can dig it." upvote. It brought my guard down- and then proceeded to veer into the best series of narrative whiplash curves I've read in months, ending on a strangely poignant and beautiful note. Don't trim a single piece- the whole is more than the sum of its parts in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it, but holy hell, laid on a little thick, wasn't it? Kids kidnapped and mutated by some freak cult, and subsequently mass-murdered…oomph, I need a damned drink.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered that as well. I understand your reason for doing so, but only what, three of them are actually important? I'd say keep them, knock the number shown in the log down to ten out of the 23, let the reader's imagination handle the rest? It could be just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an issue I was aware of, and I'm not sure how to combat it. I want to hammer home that this is 23 different children with lives and ideas and interests. I don't want it to feel like the article is favouring any one of the kids or there's "main characters" or whatever. I really wanted a solid classroom of children, because of what the incident is supposed to echo. IDK. If others find it issue with it, I'll see about changing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was just friggen' excellent. You had me going for a second with the second tab, but holy hell, did it pay off. I think you could cut most of the log of the individual instances. It was a little over-long, and I began skimming for the important bits there. You could keep the ones that are actually important, and another one or two for flavor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2338-A is an unusually large black sea nettle jellyfish3, approximately 20 foot long,

Shouldn't this be in metric?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview with Ms. Soto might be the best-written transcript I've read on this site in a very long time. I was nearly in tears myself by the end, no joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Two things that have nothing to do with each other yet their combination makes a lot of sense in context. Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is frigg'n beautiful.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spooky Scary Articles,

They send shivers down my spine…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is … oh no. I don't quite understand what went on, and yet it's tragic. I don't think learning any more would make me a happier person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Oh god.

Take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happy Hallowe'en, everyone.

I was happy with my last skip, 2559, but it had a very strict, serious tone to fit its content. I wanted to write something softer, more emotional to prove to myself I had that range. I also wanted to write it fast - 2559 took me almost a year of procrastination and 80+ edits before I posted it, and I didn't want that to be a pattern. So I set aside this weekend and told myself I had to post a good article by Hallowe'en. I started with an image that had been stuck in my head for months - a baby owl swaddled in the tight embrace of its dead octopus mother - and worked from there. The format screw came out of necessity - once I'd figured out the story I wanted to tell, it became obvious I'd need to tell it somewhat unconventionally.

Thanks to minmin, Agent MacLeod and AbsentmindedNihilist for crit. Minmin, in particular, really helped me find a shape for this and let me realise it was OK to go for a non-standard format.
